Triggers.value: Being in a natural environment similar to where it happened

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I dwell on what other people have done to me
keep mind occupied all the time

Threat.value: I only feel safe in a very limited number of places
I have difficulty falling asleep because I need to stay alert
I jump or startle at ordinary sounds that would not bother most people

Memory.value: Sometimes I can recall what happened but feel completely numb, as if it happened to a stranger
When I remember the event I feel intense shame
I know what happened but I am unable to find words to describe it

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This directly conflicts with the rest of the memory profile, especially Emotionless Recall and Shame on Recall, which indicate distressing autobiographical recall rather than easy, neutral verbal access. Within-node, the combination is not impossible but is clinically discordant enough to flag.

issues.item: Threat in Faces: I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat,
issues.explanation: This threat-monitoring item is coherent with hypervigilance, but the broader formulation has no strong Triggers→Threat edge and the Memory→Threat edge is inactive. It is still plausible clinically, but in this profile it is weakly supported relative to the other nodes and somewhat overstates a generalized threat system compared with the more trauma-cued pattern elsewhere.

vignette:
Mark, a 43-year-old married roofer of European background, has been struggling with persistent post-traumatic symptoms after a terrorist attack that occurred outdoors in a natural setting. He was working near a wooded area when the danger escalated abruptly, with thrown objects and the sudden sound of impact and shouting. In the peak moment, he recalls being exposed, unable to control what was happening around him, and having to get himself out without being able to protect others. Since then, his symptom pattern has remained tightly linked to reminders of that event: being in open natural places, seeing objects thrown, and noticing his own heart racing.

When Mark speaks about the memory, he does not tell it as a smooth story. He stops and says he can “see it, but not really feel it,” describing moments that come back as if they belong to someone else. At other times, he becomes embarrassed and visibly strained, saying he knows what happened but “can’t get the words out properly.” When he does remember, he feels intense shame, and this seems to hang over the memory rather than fade with time.

Mark’s central stuck point is that the attack confirmed a painful sense that he was powerless and somehow diminished by what happened. In his own terms, he comes close to saying that the event left him feeling “exposed, weak, and ashamed,” as though he should have done more and cannot trust his own judgment now. He also gives the impression that the world is no longer a place where he can move freely without danger.

In daily life, Mark keeps his mind occupied all the time so he does not have space to dwell on what happened. He also avoids wearing clothes that resemble what he had on that day, and he repeatedly thinks about what other people did to him. At work, as a roofer, he notices himself scanning constantly when he is on a job near trees, open ground, or construction waste that can be picked up and thrown. He startles at ordinary bangs and clatters on site, and when his heart pounds he becomes more aware of the memory and less able to settle. Nights are difficult because he feels he must stay alert, and he says there are only a very limited number of places where he feels safe enough to relax. This cycle keeps the memory close, and the memory in turn sends him back into the same reactions and protective habits, maintaining the problem.
